item: One-sided overlap tail: there exist kappa>0, t0 in (0,1), and 0<c_-<c_+ finite with c_- t^kappa <= P(propensity(X) <= t) <= c_+ t^kappa for 0<t<=t0 — a polynomial lower-tail bound on the propensity score distribution near zero.

Clipped AIPW score: the augmented inverse-probability-weighted (doubly robust) influence function for the average treatment effect, with the propensity clipped at lambda; regression difference plus the two weighted residual correction terms.

Witness KL and ATE separation: for the least-favourable pair, the per-observation Kullback-Leibler divergence is bounded so the n-fold KL stays O(1); a Bernoulli KL two-point evaluation feeding the minimax lower bound via Pinsker.

Le Cam two-point lower bound: the minimax risk is bounded below by comparing two hypotheses whose statistical divergence (chi-square or KL) is controlled — the classical two-point method for converse rates.
Backdoor adjustment: when a covariate set blocks every back-door path from treatment to outcome, the causal effect is identified by averaging the conditional outcome over the covariate distribution.

Front-door criterion: identification of a treatment effect through a fully mediating variable, chaining the treatment-to-mediator and mediator-to-outcome relations when the mediator has no unobserved confounding.
Wald / LATE estimand: with a binary instrument, the local average treatment effect on compliers equals the ratio of the instrument-outcome mean difference to the instrument-treatment mean difference.

Goodman-Bacon decomposition: the two-way fixed-effects estimator is a weighted average of all two-group two-period difference-in-differences comparisons, with weights set by group sizes and treatment timing.

Interaction-weighted event study under heterogeneous dynamic effects: the interaction-weighted estimator recovers a convex average of cohort-specific effects, unlike the contaminated two-way fixed-effects event-study coefficients.
Group-time average treatment effects: doubly robust estimands of the average effect for each treatment-timing cohort at each period, aggregated into interpretable staggered-adoption summaries.
Worst-case no-assumptions bounds: with nothing beyond the observed data and a bounded outcome, the average treatment effect is partially identified to a sharp interval.
Balke-Pearl sharp bounds: with a binary instrument, treatment, and outcome, the average treatment effect is sharply bounded via linear-programming bounds on the response-type distribution.

Randomization design over a finite assignment space: the experimental design is a probability distribution (a probability mass function) over the finite set of possible treatment assignments — the only source of randomness in a design-based analysis.
I.i.d. sampling model: n observations drawn independently from a common data-generating law P on the observation space, realized as a measurable sequence of draws.

Chi-square divergence between two measures: the integral of the squared density-ratio deviation, (density of mu over nu, minus one), squared, against nu — the chi-square statistical distance of mu from nu.

Tensorization of the chi-square divergence over an i.i.d. product: for n independent copies, one plus the chi-square divergence of the product laws factorizes into the per-coordinate factors, on a general measurable space.

Squared-loss integrability of a bounded estimator: a measurable estimator whose values are confined to a bounded range has integrable squared error, so its mean squared error is well-defined and finite.

Absolute continuity of i.i.d. product measures: if mu is absolutely continuous with respect to nu, then the n-fold product law of mu is absolutely continuous with respect to the n-fold product law of nu.

Two DAGs over the same variables impose identical conditional-independence constraints, i.e. they are Markov equivalent, exactly when they share the same skeleton and the same set of unshielded colliders (v-structures). State this graphical characterization of a DAG's Markov equivalence class.

In a semi-Markovian model, the Q-factor of any c-component (district) T is identified from the observational law by intervening on everything outside T: it coincides almost everywhere with the interventional conditional obtained under do(V \ T). Give this district-level identification result that drives the Tian–Pearl ID algorithm.

For pairwise-disjoint node sets X, Y, Z, X and Y are d-separated by Z if and only if they are separated in the moralized graph of the smallest ancestral set containing X, Y and Z — no path avoiding Z links them after moralization. State this equivalence between the directed d-separation test and undirected separation after moralizing.

Pearl's second rule of do-calculus, the action/observation exchange: for the conditional distribution of Y given W, intervening on Z has the same effect as merely observing Z whenever Y is d-separated from Z given W in the graph with the arrows out of Z deleted. Give this rule in its conditional-kernel form.

Tian's c-component factorization: the observational joint density decomposes as a product over the c-components (districts) of the latent-projection graph, one Q-factor per component. Prove that the chain-rule product of per-node observational conditionals regroups into a single factor for each c-component.

With a binary instrument that satisfies only mean independence (not full exclusion) and outcomes known to lie in a bounded range, the average treatment effect cannot be point-identified but is confined to a worst-case interval: for any pair of instrument values, one endpoint pairs the treated lower envelope at one instrument level with the control upper envelope at the other, and the opposite endpoint reverses those roles. Establish this no-selection-assumption Manski (1990) sandwich for the ATE.

Impose a monotone treatment response restriction — every unit's potential outcome is weakly increasing in treatment — on top of bounded outcomes. Show this shape restriction sign-restricts and tightens the identified set for the ATE: the effect is necessarily nonnegative, and it is bounded above by a sharp expression combining the observed factual treated and control means with the extreme admissible outcome values.

In a setting with endogenous sample selection where the outcome is observed only for a selected subpopulation, use a monotonicity (one-sided selection) assumption to identify the always-observed principal stratum and trim the treated outcome distribution at the appropriate quantile. Derive the resulting Lee (2009) sharp bounds for the average treatment effect on the always-selected group, written using the observable trimmed treated mean and the observed control mean.

The distribution of the individual treatment effect (the difference of two potential outcomes) is not identified from the two marginal outcome distributions alone, because the dependence structure is unknown. Establish the Makarov (1981) lower bound on the CDF of the difference: for any threshold, sweeping a reference point gives a valid lower bound on the probability that the difference is at most that threshold, taking the pointwise maximum over reference points against zero.

For any two random variables with fixed marginal distributions, the joint CDF is not pinned down but is constrained by the copula bounds. State the Fréchet–Hoeffding upper bound: the probability that both variables fall below their respective thresholds is at most the smaller of the two marginal CDF values.

In design-based (randomization) inference, where the assignment mechanism is the sole source of randomness, the Horvitz–Thompson estimator of the finite-population total under a fixed exposure level — each unit's outcome inverse-weighted by its own first-order exposure probability — is exactly unbiased for the true population total, as long as every unit has strictly positive probability of receiving that exposure.

The finite-population (design-based) variance of the Horvitz–Thompson total estimator written explicitly in terms of first- and second-order inclusion probabilities: a diagonal contribution π_i(1−π_i) from each unit plus off-diagonal terms (π_{ij} − π_i π_j) capturing the joint-versus-marginal exposure of unit pairs, every term scaled by the inverse-probability–weighted outcomes.

A Chebyshev concentration inequality taken over the randomization distribution of a finite experimental design: for any positive threshold, the design probability that a statistic deviates from its design expectation by at least that amount is bounded by the statistic's design variance divided by the squared threshold. This is the tail bound underlying design-based confidence intervals when no central limit theorem is invoked.

Under interference described by an exposure mapping, the studentized Horvitz–Thompson estimator of an exposure contrast satisfies a local-dependence central limit theorem, so the normal-approximation Wald confidence interval centered at the estimator and scaled by the true design standard error and the standard-normal quantile z_{1−α/2} attains asymptotic coverage of at least 1−α (Aronow–Samii network-experiment inference).

The Borusyak, Jaravel and Spiess imputation estimator for staggered adoption: fit the untreated-outcome model on the not-yet-treated and never-treated observations, use it to impute each treated cell's untreated counterfactual, and average the imputed effects. Under an additive two-way untreated-outcome model, no anticipation, and a design-side prediction-span condition, this observed-law functional identifies the weighted average of cell-level treatment-on-the-treated contrasts, i.e. the target sum a_c times E[Y(1) - Y(0) | cell c].

The Sun and Abraham interaction-weighted event-study estimator: saturate the regression in cohort-by-relative-time interaction dummies and average their coefficients within each relative-time bin using cohort-share weights. Under consistency, no anticipation, and parallel trends across the comparison groups, each such interaction-weighted difference-in-differences contrast equals the corresponding cohort-relative-time average treatment effect on the treated, so it stays free of contamination from other periods' effects.

In Goodman-Bacon's decomposition of the staggered two-way fixed-effects estimator, the building-block 2x2 comparison between a treated timing group and a never-treated (clean) control group is well behaved: under parallel trends and no anticipation, that treated-versus-never-treated difference-in-differences exactly identifies the treated group's average treatment effect on the treated over its post-adoption window, with no forbidden already-treated comparison.

A de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfoeuille style negative-weights counterexample for two-way fixed effects: because the regression assigns some treated cells negative weights, one can construct a panel in which every unit's treatment effect is strictly positive yet the TWFE coefficient comes out negative, holding the treatment assignment and residualization geometry fixed. This shows the TWFE estimand is not a convex average of heterogeneous effects and can be sign-reversed.

Wooldridge's two-way Mundlak equivalence for difference-in-differences: the scalar two-way fixed effects estimand from the within-transformed panel regression coincides with the coefficient from a pooled OLS Mundlak regression that adds unit-average and time-average covariates instead of fixed-effect dummies, provided the relevant design matrix is full rank.

With a binary instrument satisfying exclusion, independence, and monotonicity (no defiers), the local average treatment effect on the complier subpopulation is identified by the Wald ratio: the reduced-form intent-to-treat effect on the outcome divided by the first-stage effect on treatment take-up, (E[Y|Z=1] - E[Y|Z=0]) / (E[D|Z=1] - E[D|Z=0]).

Under sequential randomization (no unmeasured confounding at each successive stage), the mean counterfactual outcome under a fixed dynamic treatment regime is identified by Robins' g-formula, obtained by iterating stage-wise outcome regressions and integrating over the time-varying covariate history via backward induction.

In a two-period panel, under parallel trends and no anticipation the average treatment effect on the treated is identified by the difference-in-differences contrast: the treated group's mean pre-to-post change in outcomes minus the comparison group's mean change over the same periods.

In a sharp regression-discontinuity design, where treatment switches deterministically once the running variable crosses a threshold, the treatment effect at the cutoff is identified as the jump in the outcome regression function there: the difference between the right- and left-hand limits of E[Y | X = x] as x approaches the cutoff.

The augmented inverse-probability-weighted (doubly robust) estimator of the effect of treatment on the treated: an outcome-regression adjustment corrected by an inverse-odds-weighted residual on the control units, which recovers the ATT under unconfoundedness and one-sided overlap.
